city.name: Bangalore
quickFacts: Major tech hub with MNCs and startups
Famous for Dosa, Bisi Bele Bath

alerts.title: Single-Use Plastic Ban Enforcement
alerts.description: Karnataka strictly enforces single-use plastic bans. Using or carrying plastic bags, straws, or containers can result in heavy fines up to ₹25,000. Violators may face imprisonment up to 5 years in severe cases.

alerts.title: Helmet Mandatory for All Two-Wheeler Riders
alerts.description: Wearing a helmet is legally mandatory for all motorcycle and scooter riders in Karnataka, including passengers. Non-compliance can result in fines of ₹500-₹1,000 and traffic challan entries. Repeat offenders face license suspension.

alerts.title: Alcohol Sale Hours Restriction
alerts.description: Liquor shops in Karnataka must close between 10 PM and 6 AM. Sale of alcohol outside these hours is illegal and can result in shop closure and fines. Consumption in public spaces is also prohibited.

alerts.title: Noise Pollution Regulations
alerts.description: Noise levels must not exceed 55dB (day) and 45dB (night) in residential areas of Bangalore. Violations during construction, events, or public gatherings can result in fines and equipment seizure. Always check local noise regulations before events.

alerts.title: Public Smoking Prohibition
alerts.description: Smoking is prohibited in all public spaces including parks, public transport, restaurants, and workplaces in Karnataka. Violators face fines of ₹200 for first offense and up to ₹500 for repeat violations. Spitting in public is also illegal.

alerts.title: Speed Limit Enforcement on Highways
alerts.description: Speed limits on NH44 and NH66 are strictly 80 km/h for cars and 60 km/h for commercial vehicles. Exceeding limits results in fines starting from ₹400. Extreme speeding (over 100 km/h) can lead to vehicle impoundment and license cancellation.

alerts.title: Driving License Requirements
alerts.description: International driving licenses must be registered with local RTO within 30 days of entry. Driving without a valid license attracts fines of ₹1,000 and potential vehicle impoundment. Always carry your license and vehicle registration documents.

alerts.title: Temple Etiquette and Sacred Practices
alerts.description: When entering Karnataka temples, remove shoes, show respect to deities, and avoid pointing at sacred objects. Women should wear modest clothing covering shoulders and knees. Photography is often prohibited in sanctums. Always seek permission before photographing rituals.

alerts.title: Vijayanagara Empire Heritage Sites Protocol
alerts.description: Historic sites like Hampi and Badami require respectful behavior. Avoid touching ancient sculptures and inscriptions, stay on designated paths, and hire official guides for detailed tours. Breaking artifacts or vandalism results in severe legal penalties and cultural disrespect.

alerts.title: Diwali and Festival Celebration Norms
alerts.description: During Diwali and other festivals, firecrackers are restricted to specific hours (usually 6-8 PM). Many areas of Bangalore have banned firecrackers entirely. Respect noise restrictions and participate in community celebrations respectfully. Public celebrations are more welcoming than private bursting.

alerts.title: Food and Drink Customs
alerts.description: Eating with your right hand is customary in Karnataka. Beef is sensitive in many communities; vegetarian options are widely respected. Offering or eating food with left hand can be considered disrespectful. Coffee culture is strong - refusal of coffee might be seen as rude in social settings.

alerts.title: Kannada Language Respect
alerts.description: Kannada is the official language of Karnataka. While English and Hindi are widely spoken in Bangalore, respecting and learning basic Kannada phrases shows cultural appreciation. Many locals appreciate efforts to speak Kannada, even if imperfect.

alerts.title: Marriage and Family Customs
alerts.description: Karnataka has diverse cultural communities with different wedding customs. Accepting invitations warmly and respecting religious ceremonies is important. Gift-giving is customary at weddings. Avoid scheduling important events during major festival seasons.

alerts.title: Interaction with Elders
alerts.description: Respect for elders is paramount in Karnataka culture. Use respectful titles like 'Appa' or 'Amma', avoid direct disagreement, and always greet elders first. Standing when elders enter a room and offering them the best seat is customary.

alerts.title: Coffee House Etiquette
alerts.description: Bangalore's famous coffee houses are social spaces. Coffee drinking is a leisurely activity, not rushed. Tipping is appreciated but not mandatory (5-10% is standard). It's common to spend hours in coffee shops; rushing customers away is rare.

alerts.title: Auto-Rickshaw Negotiation Protocol
alerts.description: Always negotiate auto-rickshaw fares before boarding, especially outside tourist areas or late night. Insist on the meter if the driver refuses. Meter fare is approximately ₹40 base + ₹30/km. Avoid traveling alone late night; use registered taxis or ride-sharing apps for safety.

alerts.title: Traffic and Road Safety Behavior
alerts.description: Traffic in Bangalore can be chaotic; always stay alert. Crossing streets requires careful attention - look both ways even on one-way roads. Avoid walking alone at night in unfamiliar areas. Respect one-way directions and lane markings. Honking is common but not aggressive.

alerts.title: Water Safety in Rivers and Lakes
alerts.description: Many rivers in Karnataka (Krishna, Tungabhadra) have strong currents and unpredictable water levels. Swimming without local guidance is dangerous. Always ask locals about safety before swimming. Avoid water during monsoon season (June-September) when levels are high and currents are strong.

alerts.title: Tipping Customs and Restaurant Etiquette
alerts.description: Tipping 5-10% is standard in restaurants and cafes, though not mandatory. Service charge is often added automatically (check your bill). For auto-rickshaws, rounding up the fare (₹5-10) is appreciated. Hotel staff and service workers appreciate tips. Never tip in government establishments.

alerts.title: Dress Code and Weather Adaptation
alerts.description: Bangalore's weather is mild year-round (15-30°C). Light cotton clothing is ideal. During monsoon (June-September), carry an umbrella and wear waterproof shoes. In temples and conservative areas, avoid sleeveless tops and shorts. Smart casual is appropriate for most social settings and workplaces.
